agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving significant ambiguity around thresholds, guardrails, and operational details.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity in a given area and time window — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or unreasonable pricing — thresholds undefined" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "Implied but not stated — the starting delivery price before dynamic adjustments are applied" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "The delta applied to a delivery price as a result of demand conditions — direction and magnitude governed by DynaPricing rules" },
      { "term": "Real Time", "definition": "Price changes are applied to incoming orders as demand conditions change, not batched or scheduled" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must be adjusted based on current demand levels — higher demand produces higher prices, lower demand produces lower prices",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy. A VP could change the pricing strategy (e.g., flat pricing during promotions, demand-insensitive pricing for VIP customers). It is a business policy, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand level D1 > D2 in the same zone and time, the price offered at D1 must be greater than the price offered at D2"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — the price presented to a customer must reflect demand conditions at the moment of order placement, not stale or batched data",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time is a latency policy. The business could decide near-real-time (e.g., 5-minute windows) is acceptable. This is an SLA, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "The demand signal used to compute a delivery price must have been sampled no more than N seconds before order placement (N is undefined in spec)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The pricing system must be fully automated — no manual human approval is required for individual price adjustments",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation level is an operational policy. Management could revert to manual overrides or approval gates at any time.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are applied without any human-in-the-loop approval step during normal operation"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from reaching extreme values — there must be an upper bound and a lower bound on the price or the adjustment multiplier",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without guardrails, the system could produce zero-dollar or astronomically high prices, corrupting order data and violating contractual/regulatory obligations. A system that can output a $0 or $999,999 delivery fee is in a corrupt state. The existence of bounds is an integrity constraint even if the specific values are policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed dynamic price P: P >= PRICE_FLOOR and P <= PRICE_CEILING, where PRICE_FLOOR > 0 and PRICE_CEILING is a defined finite value"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The demand-to-price function must be monotonically non-decreasing — price must never decrease as demand increases",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If higher demand produced lower prices, the system would be in a logically inconsistent state that contradicts its fundamental purpose. This is a mathematical invariant of the pricing function itself — violating it means the algorithm is corrupt, not that a policy changed.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any two demand values where D_a > D_b, computePrice(D_a) >= computePrice(D_b), all other inputs held equal"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The system must balance competitiveness and profitability — dynamic prices must not fall below the cost of fulfillment (profitability floor) and must remain within market-competitive range",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a strategic business decision that can shift. The company might choose to operate at a loss to gain market share. This is a policy, not an integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Dynamic price >= cost of fulfillment for the delivery (exact cost model undefined in spec)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "A delivery must have exactly one price at the moment of customer commitment — the price shown and accepted is the price charged",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the price changes between customer acceptance and charge, the system has violated transactional integrity. This is a fundamental e-commerce and contractual invariant — you cannot charge a different price than what was agreed upon.",
      "testable_assertion": "The price recorded on a confirmed order must equal the price displayed to the customer at the moment of order confirmation"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Price adjustments apply per-delivery — each delivery request is priced individually based on current conditions at the time of that request",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The business could introduce batch pricing, subscription models, or locked-rate contracts. Per-delivery pricing is the current policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Two delivery requests placed at different times in the same zone may receive different prices if demand conditions differ"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., concert ends, weather event)", "expected_behavior": "Prices rise but are capped by the upper guardrail; the system does not overshoot into predatory pricing territory" },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price falls to the floor guardrail value but never below cost of fulfillment and never to zero" },
    { "scenario": "Customer views price, then confirms order seconds later when demand has shifted", "expected_behavior": "The price-lock policy must be defined — either the quoted price is honored for a TTL window, or the customer sees the updated price before confirming. Spec does not clarify." },
    { "scenario": "System outage or demand signal unavailable", "expected_behavior": "Fallback pricing behavior is undefined. System must have a degradation mode (e.g., revert to base price) to avoid either blocking orders or pricing blind." },
    { "scenario": "Two simultaneous orders in same zone see different prices due to race condition", "expected_behavior": "Each order must be priced on the demand snapshot at its own commitment time — minor differences are acceptable, but prices must always respect guardrails" },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws triggered by surge pricing during emergencies", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails must account for legal maximums in jurisdictions where price-gouging statutes apply — spec is silent on this" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact: dynamic pricing can increase revenue 10-30% during high-demand periods, but risks customer backlash and regulatory exposure if guardrails are poorly defined. Profitability depends entirely on floor pricing being above fulfillment cost.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand signal infrastructure, pricing engine with sub-second latency, and monitoring/alerting for guardrail breaches. Fully automated means no manual safety net — failure modes must be pre-defined.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price shock during high demand is the #1 UX risk. Positive: lower prices during off-peak may increase adoption.",
    "risk": "HIGH — vague guardrails are the single biggest risk. Without defined bounds, the system could produce legally or reputationally damaging prices. Regulatory risk in jurisdictions with price-gouging laws. Reputational risk from perceived unfairness.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Variable pricing affects trust, affordability, and willingness to order during peak times" },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Higher prices during demand surges may increase courier earnings and availability, but floor prices may compress margins" },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue", "impact": "Revenue optimization opportunity but requires accurate cost modeling to ensure profitability floor holds" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Price-gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and contractual obligations must be evaluated per jurisdiction" },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability — needs clear escalation paths and refund policies" },
      { "group": "Product/Marketing", "impact": "Must communicate pricing model transparently to avoid backlash; competitive positioning changes" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is an informal all-hands statement, not a specification. It contains no concrete thresholds, no defined guardrails, no demand measurement methodology, no pricing formula, no jurisdictional considerations, and hedges with 'probably' on the most critical safety mechanism (guardrails). Nearly every operational detail is left undefined. This is a vision statement, not a spec.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices increase when demand is high (BR-1) — explicitly stated",
      "Prices decrease when demand is low (BR-1) — explicitly stated",
      "System is automated (BR-3) — explicitly stated",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (BR-4) — stated, though hedged with 'probably'"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Guardrail thresholds — completely undefined, hedged with 'probably'",
      "Definition of 'demand' — no measurement methodology specified",
      "Definition of 'real time' — no latency SLA",
      "What 'competitive' means — no market benchmarks defined",
      "What 'profitable' means — no cost model or margin targets",
      "Price-lock behavior during order flow",
      "Fallback behavior during system degradation",
      "Regulatory/jurisdictional constraints",
      "Whether guardrails are per-zone, per-time, or global"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base price exists that adjustments are applied to — not stated but implied",
      "Demand is measured at a geographic zone level — not stated",
      "Price-lock integrity at order confirmation is required — standard e-commerce invariant, not stated",
      "Guardrails include both upper and lower bounds — only 'prices don't go crazy' (upward) was mentioned",
      "The pricing function is continuous and monotonic — implied by 'high demand = high price' but not explicitly required"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrails are described as 'probably' existing with no defined thresholds. This is the most critical safety mechanism in the system and it is both uncertain and undefined. We cannot write acceptance criteria without knowing: (a) Do guardrails definitely exist? (b) What are the upper/lower bounds? (c) Are they absolute values or multipliers? (d) Are they global or per-zone?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance/Revenue"],
      "options": ["Define hard price floor and ceiling as absolute values", "Define guardrails as multiplier caps on base price (e.g., max 3x)", "Define per-zone guardrails based on local market conditions"],
      "recommendation": "Require product/legal to define concrete guardrail thresholds before any implementation begins. This is non-negotiable for launch."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No definition of how 'demand' is measured. Is it order volume per hour? Orders vs available couriers? Geographic density? Without this, the entire pricing function is undefined.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product/Marketing", "Couriers/Drivers", "Finance/Revenue"],
      "options": ["Orders per hour in a zone", "Ratio of orders to available couriers", "Estimated wait time as demand proxy"],
      "recommendation": "Define demand as the ratio of active delivery requests to available courier capacity in a given zone and time window. This is the most operationally meaningful signal."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory or legal review mentioned. Dynamic/surge pricing is regulated in many jurisdictions, especially during emergencies. Price-gouging laws could make the system illegal in certain conditions without proper caps.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Proceed without legal review", "Block launch until legal review per jurisdiction"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review of price-gouging statutes in all operating jurisdictions must be completed before guardrail values are finalized."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No price-lock or quote TTL behavior defined. When a customer sees a price and then confirms, demand may have changed. The spec does not say whether the quoted price is honored or recalculated at confirmation time.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Finance/Revenue"],
      "options": ["Lock price for a TTL (e.g., 2 minutes) after quote", "Re-price at confirmation and show updated price", "Lock price at quote but cap maximum increase at confirmation"],
      "recommendation": "Lock the quoted price for a short TTL (60-120 seconds) to prevent customer frustration and cart abandonment. Re-quote if TTL expires."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
